Conflict overview

Ukraine-Russia conflict — history, drivers, current state, and opportunities

Methodological noteThis is a multi-causal conflict. It cannot be explained by any single factor — not NATO expansion alone, not Russian imperialism alone, not ancient ethnic hatreds. Each driver played a role. This overview holds them in tension rather than offering false clarity through a single explanation.
Five drivers — held simultaneously
01
Geopolitical competition
Russia determined to prevent Ukraine joining NATO or EU — seen as an existential threat.
02
Identity and nationhood
The contested question of whether Ukraine is a distinct nation — answered definitively in February 2022.
03
Institutional failure
Thirty years of endemic corruption and oscillating foreign policy left Ukraine vulnerable.
04
Economic interests
Russian gas leverage, Ukraine as energy transit, EU integration stakes — all economic dimensions.
05
Security guarantee failure
The 1994 Budapest Memorandum — Ukraine gave up its nuclear arsenal for assurances that proved worthless.
What a settlement needs to address
IssueThe challengeStatus
Territorial questionNo formula currently satisfies both sides. Russia wants recognition; Ukraine cannot concede.Zero-sum
Security guaranteesBudapest failure means any new guarantees must be credible — substance over paper.Latent ZOPA
Economic reconstructionUkraine needs $500bn+. Who pays — and on what conditions — is central.Convergent
War crimes accountabilityZelensky's stated requirement. Putin's existential red line.Zero-sum
Sanctions normalizationRussia needs a path back to economic engagement. This is leverage.Negotiable
Future security architectureNATO, EU, new framework? Ukraine's institutional future is inseparable from the conflict's causes.Latent ZOPA

Conflict timeline

Ukraine path (blue) · Russia path (rust) · Converging moments where both paths meet · Click any event to expand

Ukraine path
Russia path
Convergence — both parties
Crisis / escalation
Diplomacy
Conflict
Political shift
← Ukraine
Russia →
Political
1932–33
The Holodomor
Soviet-engineered famine kills 3.5–7M Ukrainians
Stalin's regime deliberately starves the Ukrainian countryside to break nationalism. Two brothers dead before Putin was born; Ukrainian families across generations carry this memory. For Ukraine, this is the foundation of the conviction that Russian domination is existential, not merely political.
Why it matters: Not background — the foundation of Ukrainian national identity.
Both parties
1954
Crimea transferred to Ukraine
Khrushchev moves Crimea from Russia to Ukraine within the Soviet Union
A largely administrative act within a unified Soviet state. Nobody in 1954 imagined it would become the flashpoint for the worst European war since 1945. The transfer is Russia's legal basis for calling Crimea "always Russian" — and Ukraine's legal basis for calling it "always ours." Both claims are simultaneously true and irreconcilable.
Why it matters: A bureaucratic decision becomes the central territorial dispute of the 21st century.
Both parties
1991
Ukrainian independence — 92%
Soviet Union collapses. Ukraine votes overwhelmingly for independence, including majorities in eastern Ukraine and Crimea.
The referendum was not close, not merely western Ukrainian, and not imposed. 92% of Ukrainian citizens — including in Crimea — chose independence. Ukraine also inherits the world's third-largest nuclear arsenal almost by accident. Russia experiences this as the loss of the "Russian world's" heartland.
Why it matters: The democratic legitimacy of Ukrainian independence is overwhelming. This is not a western Ukrainian project.
Diplomacy Both parties
1994
Budapest Memorandum
Ukraine gives up nuclear weapons in exchange for security assurances from Russia, US, and UK.
The most consequential broken promise of the post-Cold War era. Ukraine transfers the world's third-largest nuclear arsenal. In return, signatories commit to "security assurances" — carefully chosen language that avoids "guarantees" and implies no military defense obligation. When Russia violates its commitments in 2014 and 2022, the consultation mechanism is invoked three times. Nothing happens.
Why it matters: Every discussion of security guarantees for Ukraine is permanently shadowed by this failure.
Political
2004
Orange Revolution
Mass protests overturn a fraudulent election. Viktor Yushchenko elected on pro-European platform.
Ukrainian civil society discovers its collective capacity for action. Russia calls it a Western-backed coup. Ukraine calls it a democratic choice. Both readings contain truth. The outcome is disappointing — the coalition falls apart — but the pattern is established: when pushed too far, Ukrainians mobilize.
Why it matters: First major inflection toward Europe. Establishes the pattern that Maidan will follow.
Diplomacy
2008
NATO Bucharest Summit
US pushes for Ukraine and Georgia NATO membership. Germany and France block it. Communiqué says Ukraine "will become" a member — without a timeline.
The worst of both worlds. Enough to alarm Moscow, not enough to deter it. Putin warns publicly this would be "a hostile act." Four months later, Russia invades Georgia. The gap between the NATO promise and NATO commitment becomes the strategic ambiguity that will haunt the next fourteen years.
Why it matters: Sets the template for strategic ambiguity that encouraged Russia and failed to protect Ukraine.
Political
2013–14
Maidan revolution
Yanukovych rejects EU agreement, pivots to Russia. Mass protests erupt. After months of confrontation and sniper fire, Yanukovych flees to Russia.
Three months of growing protests, government violence, and 100+ deaths culminate in the president's flight. Russia calls it a coup engineered by the West. Ukraine calls it a revolution. The underlying reality: a corrupt president abandoned an EU agreement his government had spent years negotiating, in favor of a Russian economic package. The Ukrainian people said no.
Why it matters: The proximate trigger for everything that follows.
Conflict
March 2014
Russia annexes Crimea
"Little green men" — unmarked Russian troops — seize Crimea. A staged referendum. Formal annexation.
The first annexation of a European state's territory by force since World War II. Russia calls it a protection of Russian speakers and a response to the Western-backed coup in Kyiv. The international community condemns it but imposes only limited costs. Putin reads this as confirmation that the West will not respond forcefully to territorial revision.
Why it matters: The decisive legal event. The international community's failure to impose meaningful costs is, in many analysts' view, the proximate cause of 2022.
Diplomacy Both parties
2015
Minsk II Agreement
Second attempt to manage the Donbas conflict. Both sides later acknowledge it was primarily tactical.
Ukraine needed time to build its military. Europe needed time before it was strong enough to face Russia. Russia was buying time to consolidate gains. Nobody was negotiating in good faith. Both Zelensky and Merkel later confirmed this. Minsk II became a template for how each side views subsequent negotiations: as tactical maneuver rather than genuine settlement architecture.
Why it matters: The bad-faith negotiation pattern shapes how all subsequent talks are interpreted.
Political
2019
Zelensky elected with 73%
The comedian-actor who played an honest president on TV runs as that character and wins in a landslide.
Volodymyr Zelensky — comedian, actor, creator of Servant of the People — wins with 73% against incumbent Petro Poroshenko. He has never held political office. The gap between the character he played and the president he would become would only become apparent on February 24, 2022, when he walked the streets of Kyiv at night with his phone, refusing the US offer of evacuation.
Why it matters: The president who will lead Ukraine through the war is elected. His formation — comedy, reading rooms, Jewish identity — turns out to be exactly the preparation needed.
Political
July 2021
Putin publishes "On the Historical Unity"
7,000-word essay arguing Russians and Ukrainians are "one people" and Ukraine is an artificial construction.
The ideological blueprint for the invasion, published seven months before it. Putin argues that Ukraine as a separate nation is a product of Lenin-era Soviet policy and Western manipulation. This is not improvisation or propaganda — it is a considered historical argument that reveals how Putin actually understands the conflict. He genuinely believes this. That is what makes it so dangerous.
Why it matters: Reveals the ideological framework behind the invasion. Not a pretext — a conviction.
Conflict
Feb 24, 2022
"The fight is here. I need ammunition, not a ride."
Zelensky refuses evacuation and walks the streets of Kyiv at night. Ukraine holds.
The US offered to evacuate Zelensky from Kyiv. His response became the defining sentence of the war. He understood exactly what the moment required — not bravery for its own sake, but clarity about what leadership meant in that specific moment. The Kyiv offensive, designed to take the capital in days, failed within three weeks. Russia's military reputation did not survive the opening act.
Why it matters: The founding moment of the wartime Zelensky. Everything that followed built on this.
Conflict
Feb 24, 2022
Full-scale invasion
Russia launches simultaneous attacks from north, east, and south. The Kyiv assault — designed to take the capital in days — fails.
Conflict
September 2022
Russia annexes four oblasts — "forever"
Putin formally annexes Donetsk, Luhansk, Zaporizhzhia, and Kherson. None fully controlled militarily. Declares them "forever" Russian.
The word "forever" is the analytical key. It is not a negotiating position but a statement of ontological fact. Any settlement that acknowledges Ukrainian sovereignty over these territories is, within Russia's framework, asking Russia to abandon its own people. Putin has made the territorial question as hard as it is possible to make it.
Why it matters: "Forever" closes the diplomatic door. This is why agreed ambiguity — not formal recognition — is the only formula that has a chance.
Political
June 2023
Wagner mutiny — Prigozhin killed
Prigozhin leads Wagner toward Moscow. Turns back. Dies in a plane crash two months later.
Reveals fissures in Putin's control of military and paramilitary forces. The internal stability of the Russian state is not as solid as it appears from outside. Putin's response — tactical retreat followed by assassination — reveals his operating method when challenged internally. The Gaddafi fear is visible in how he responds to Prigozhin.
Why it matters: The first public evidence of internal fracture within the Russian military ecosystem.
Political
November 2024
Trump elected — second term
The most consequential external development for the conflict's trajectory since the invasion itself.
Trump has called Putin "smart" and "savvy," expressed admiration for his Ukraine moves, and promised to end the war in 24 hours. He has now been president for over a year and has not ended it. The gap between the promise and the reality is the space in which a negotiator can work — if they give him the story of how he did it.
Why it matters: Changes the Western support calculus fundamentally. Creates urgency. Also creates opportunity.
Diplomacy
May 2026
72-hour ceasefire expires
US-brokered ceasefire announced via Truth Social. Both sides accuse each other of violations. We are here now.
Putin marks Victory Day with a scaled-back parade and hints the war may be "coming to an end." The 72-hour ceasefire expires without structural agreement. European officials debate engaging Moscow directly. The window is open — or may be about to close.
Why it matters: This is the current moment. The question this tool is designed to help answer.
Diplomacy
May 9, 2026
"The matter is coming to an end"
Putin's Victory Day address — the most explicit exit-ramp signal he has sent. Rehabilitates Istanbul 2022 as the face-saving narrative.

Victory speeches

Five leaders · Five victories · One settlement · Written in each leader's voice

Each speech is written as if delivered on the same day to each leader's own audience. The guiding question: not "what does this leader want?" but "how can this leader come out looking like a hero to the people they care most about?" Click any speech to read the full version.

Vladimir Putin
President of Russia
Russia stood firm · The world respects us again · Our future is secure
Russia stood firm
  • We protected our people and our civilization from those who sought to destroy them.
  • Russia was not defeated. Russia was not humiliated. Russia did not break.
  • What we set out to protect, we protected — and history will record that we were right.
The world respects us again
  • Russia sits at the table of great powers — not as a supplicant, but as an equal.
  • No settlement was reached without Russia's agreement. No line was drawn without Russia's voice.
Our future is secure
  • The threat to Russia's western border has been addressed. Our red lines have been respected.
  • Russia will now build — on its own terms — the future our soldiers made possible.
Volodymyr Zelensky
President of Ukraine
Ukraine survived · Ukraine chose Europe · Ukraine will rise
Ukraine survived
  • On February 24, 2022, they came to erase us. We are still here.
  • Ukraine did not fall. Ukraine did not surrender. We gave them our faces, not our backs.
Ukraine chose Europe
  • Ukraine's place in the European family of nations is no longer a question — it is a fact being built.
  • What was taken was not surrendered. Our position in international law is unchanged.
Ukraine will rise
  • To those we lost — this is your legacy. Ukraine lives because you chose not to let it die.
  • To our children: you will grow up in a Ukraine that is free, safe, and European.
Donald Trump
President of the United States
The deal of the century · America First · I did what no one else could
The deal of the century
  • Three years. Every expert said impossible. I did it.
  • Biden couldn't. Obama couldn't. I got it done.
America First, always
  • America is not writing Europe's security check anymore.
I did what no one else could
  • The guns are silent. History will remember this.
Emmanuel Macron
President of France
Europe was decisive · France led · The vision was right
Europe was decisive
  • For the first time in a generation, Europe acted with its own forces and will.
  • The sceptics said strategic autonomy was a vanity project. They were wrong.
The vision was right
  • In 2017 I said Europe must become a sovereign power. Today that is a reality being built.
Friedrich Merz
Chancellor of Germany
Germany was there · Zeitenwende real · Trusted again
Germany was there
  • Germany was there. Not watching. Not deliberating. Not abstaining. There.
We are trusted again
  • Poland trusts us more. The Baltic states trust us more. Ukraine trusts us more.
  • We did not forget where we came from. And because we did not forget, we acted differently.

Harmony check

Five speeches, five audiences, one settlement. No speech requires any other leader to have lost. The one structural tension — Trump says he brokered the deal; Macron says Europe led — is resolved: "America provided the leverage. Europe built the architecture."

Verdict: The set is harmonious ✓

Leader profiles

Click any leader for the full narrative profile — formation, interests, identity needs, BATNA, and deepest fear

Vladimir Putin
Russia · Age 72
Position
Recognize territorial gains · No NATO · Security guarantees
Underlying interest
End war without looking defeated · Restore great-power status
Identity need
Not be humiliated · Reverse the 1990s · Avoid the Gaddafi scenario
Deepest fear
Humiliation. The crowd at the gate with nobody coming.
Formed by: Leningrad siege · Dresden 1989 "Moscow is silent" · KGB 16 years · Judo philosophy · Ivan Ilyin
Volodymyr Zelensky
Ukraine · Age 47
Position
Full territorial restoration · NATO equivalent · War crimes tribunal
Underlying interest
Settlement parliament and public can accept · Real deterrence
Identity need
President who saved Ukraine's existence as a nation
Deepest fear
Signing a settlement his own people judge as capitulation.
Formed by: Kryvyi Rih · Jewish family, Holocaust memory · KVN comedy · Servant of the People · "I need ammunition, not a ride"
Donald Trump
United States · Age 78
Position
End the war quickly · Stop where you are · Reduce US burden
Underlying interest
The deal no one else could make · Art of the Deal at history's scale
Identity need
Greatest dealmaker in history · Credit architecture matters most
Deepest fear
A deal nobody credits him with — or one that collapses after he claimed it.
Formed by: Queens vs. Manhattan · Fred Trump "Win, be killers" · NYMA rules of dominance · Art of the Deal as total worldview
Emmanuel Macron
France · Age 48
Position
No concessions · European leadership · Ukrainian sovereignty
Underlying interest
Settlement proves European strategic autonomy works
Identity need
Statesman who held European civilization together at its most dangerous moment
Deepest fear
The war ends in a Washington deal where Europe was consulted but not decisive.
Formed by: Amiens · Self-chosen baptism at 12 · Ricoeur's philosophy · "En même temps" as method
Friedrich Merz
Germany · Age 70
Position
No reward for aggression · Multilateral guarantees · International law
Underlying interest
Validates Zeitenwende · Germany as reliable security partner
Identity need
Vindication after 20 years of being right about Russia
Deepest fear
Being the chancellor whose actions contributed to an escalation that went wrong.
Formed by: Sauerland Catholic conservatism · Nazi-era family shadow · Merkel exile · BlackRock years

Zone of possible agreement

Overlap matrix
Issue areaPutinZelenskyTrumpMacron / MerzOverlap
End the warNeeds drain to stopStop dying — not at any priceNeeds deal urgentlyStop budget drain✓ Convergent
SecurityNo NATO — buffer zoneNATO-equivalent deterrenceReduce US burdenEuropean-led guarantee~ Latent ZOPA
TerritoryFormal recognitionFull restoration — stated. Flexible — privateA number on the mapNo formal recognition✗ Zero-sum
Face-savingRussia not defeatedUkraine not surrenderedTrump the dealmakerEurope decisive~ Latent ZOPA
ReconstructionSanctions liftedMassive aid neededReduced US burdenEU integration✓ Convergent
Historical legacyReversed 1990sSaved Ukraine's existenceGreatest dealmakerSaved European civilization~ Latent ZOPA
Four-pillar settlement architecture
Pillar 1
Ceasefire and separation of forces
PutinFighting stops. Forces hold territory.
ZelenskyThe dying stops. Sovereignty claim preserved.
TrumpHe ended the war. The guns are silent.
EuropeBasis for reconstruction and engagement.
Pillar 2
European peace and security presence
PutinNot NATO. Not a forward base.
ZelenskyCredible deterrence. Armed. Will respond.
TrumpEuropeans paying for their own security.
EuropeStrategic autonomy made operational.
Pillar 3
Agreed ambiguity on territory
PutinDe facto control preserved.
ZelenskyNo formal recognition. Sovereignty intact in law.
TrumpA number on the map. A deal he can claim.
EuropeInternational law not formally violated.
Pillar 4
Reconstruction and normalization
PutinPath to sanctions relief.
ZelenskyResources to rebuild. EU membership path.
TrumpEurope and frozen assets pay. US business access.
EuropeEU integration of Ukraine proceeds.
What the ZOPA does not resolve
The war crimes question
Zelensky requires an international tribunal. Putin cannot agree to any mechanism that could indict himself. Most likely deferred, not resolved.
The Crimea problem
Annexed in 2014. Putin calls it "sacred." The agreed-ambiguity formula faces a harder test here than on the 2022 territories.
The Trump volatility problem
Any settlement requiring sustained US commitment is structurally fragile. The European architecture is partly designed to address this.
The eastern flank problem
Poland, the Baltic states, Finland — a settlement they regard as appeasement will face active institutional resistance.

Spoilers map

Actors who benefit from conflict continuation and actively work against settlement

What is a spoiler?An actor who believes peace threatens their power, position, or interests — and uses political pressure or violence to undermine peace processes. Spoilers are not simply people who oppose the current settlement terms; they are actors who benefit from the conflict continuing and have capacity to act on that interest.
Russian siloviki hardliners
Internal spoiler — Russian state
Security and intelligence establishment who have staked institutional credibility on the war achieving its stated goals. A settlement that looks like Russian defeat would be internally destabilizing for Putin.
Interest: Power and institutional survival tied to the war narrative
Russian defense industry
Economic spoiler — Russian
Defense contractors and industrialists profiting from wartime production contracts. Economic interests aligned with conflict continuation.
Interest: Wartime production revenues, government contracts
Ukrainian military hardliners
Internal spoiler — Ukrainian
Senior military figures who have publicly committed to full territorial restoration and regard any compromise as betrayal. An armed society with battle-hardened units is a structural constraint on Zelensky's flexibility.
Interest: Institutional identity built on full restoration narrative
Eastern European maximalists
Regional spoiler — NATO allies
Poland, Baltic states, and other Eastern European NATO members who regard any territorial concession as setting a precedent for Russian revisionism that threatens their own security.
Interest: Security precedent — no reward for territorial aggression
Iranian regime
External spoiler — regional
Iran has supplied Russia with Shahed drones and has an interest in the conflict continuing to tie down Western attention and provide a market for Iranian military technology.
Interest: Strategic distraction, drone export revenue, Russia relationship
Far-right Ukrainian nationalists
Internal spoiler — Ukrainian society
Political and paramilitary actors for whom any territorial compromise represents betrayal of Ukrainian identity and the war dead. Have capacity to destabilize Ukrainian politics.
Interest: National identity, political power, ideological commitment
Russian Orthodox Church (Kirill)
Ideological spoiler — Russian
Patriarch Kirill has provided theological justification for the war as a "holy war." The Church has institutional interest in the civilizational framing — a pragmatic peace deal undercuts the narrative.
Interest: Civilizational narrative, institutional role, state proximity
Western defense industry
Economic spoiler — Western
Defense manufacturers whose revenues have grown significantly as a result of the conflict. Create structural economic interests in conflict continuation within political systems with significant defense industry lobbying.
Interest: Production revenues, long-term defense contracts
Trump MAGA maximalists
Internal spoiler — US political
Elements of Trump's base who oppose any continued US involvement or any settlement that doesn't humiliate Russia. Their pressure constrains Trump's flexibility and creates domestic risks for any deal he reaches.
Interest: Political identity, domestic leverage over Trump

Winning & blocking coalition tracker

Mapping actors on the spectrum from blocking to supporting a settlement — and who is moveable

⚠ This component is in development. Actor placement is indicative, not definitive. The scoring methodology requires further development.
The frameworkEvery actor sits somewhere on a spectrum from actively blocking a settlement to actively supporting one. The key insight: you build a winning coalition partly by moving actors out of the blocking coalition — not by persuading them the settlement is good, but by addressing the specific interest that makes them a blocker.
Coalition spectrum — indicative placement

(cautious)
The moveable actors
ActorCurrent positionWhat makes them a blockerWhat would move them
Eastern European NATOConditionalFear that any territorial concession sets a precedent threatening their own securityRobust NATO security guarantees for themselves + credible enforcement mechanism in Ukraine settlement
Russian silovikiBlockingInstitutional credibility staked on war achieving stated goalsSettlement framed as Russian victory domestically — face-saving narrative must work for this audience first
Ukrainian military hardlinersConditionalRegard territorial compromise as betrayal of war deadCredible armed security presence + clear path to future territorial recovery via international law
ChinaCautious neutralDoesn't want to alienate Russia or be seen taking Western sideSettlement that preserves Russian-Chinese relationship while reducing reputational risk of continued war association

Statements tracker

Key quotes · Shift analysis · Vocabulary patterns — the language bank for victory speeches · Updated May 14, 2026

The tracker serves two functions: intelligence (is the ZOPA opening or closing?) and vocabulary (the language bank for victory speeches). Every speech must sound like the leader — their actual words and rhythms are sourced here.
Theme 1 — On the nature of the war
Putin· May 9, 2026 · Victory Day address
"The matter is coming to an end. We agreed in Istanbul in 2022. The agreement was initialed. Then they told us it was unfair — and the confrontation continues to this day."
Analyst note
Significant. "Coming to an end" is the most explicit exit-ramp signal Putin has sent. The rehabilitation of Istanbul 2022 as Western-sabotaged is the face-saving narrative being constructed in real time. He is building the story he needs to end this as non-defeat.
Zelensky· February 24, 2022 · Night video address, Kyiv streets
"We did not raise the white flag. We began to defend the blue and yellow. We were not afraid, we did not break down, we did not surrender."
Analyst note
The founding statement. Note the three-part denial structure — "did not / did not / did not" — it recurs throughout his wartime rhetoric. This is a key vocabulary pattern for the victory speech: "Russia was not defeated. Russia was not humiliated. Russia did not break." mirrors Zelensky's own founding cadence.
Trump· February 23, 2022 · Fox Business interview
"I went in yesterday and there was a television screen, and I said, 'This is genius.' Putin declares a big portion of Ukraine — of Ukraine — Putin declares it as independent. How smart is that?"
Analyst note
The "genius" quote. Said one day before the full-scale invasion. Trump reading Putin through the Art of the Deal lens: if you can do it and get away with it, it is smart. The opening position from which everything developed.
Theme 3 — On negotiations and territorial questions
Trump· October 17, 2025 · Truth Social
"Enough blood has been shed, with property lines being defined by War and Guts. Ukraine and Russia should STOP at the battle line, make a deal and start rebuilding their nations!"
Analyst note
"Property lines defined by War and Guts" — the most revealing phrase in Trump's Ukraine lexicon. Treats national borders as the outcome of a real estate dispute. His most openly pro-Russian territorial statement.
Zelensky· October 2025 · Response to Trump "stop where you are" post
"The president is right we have to stop where we are, and then to speak. They attacked us, so they are an enemy for us."
Analyst note
Significant shift. First public acceptance of "stop where you are" as a framework — partial validation of the ceasefire-line approach. But the second half immediately complicates it. He is simultaneously accepting the ceasefire concept and expressing deep skepticism.
Theme 4 — On Europe's role vs. America's role
Merz· February 14, 2026 · Munich Security Conference
"My absolute priority will be to strengthen Europe as quickly as possible so that, step by step, we can really achieve independence from the USA."
Analyst note
"Independence from the USA" — from the most transatlanticist chancellor Germany had produced in a generation. Trump pushed him here. The convergence of Macron and Merz on this language at MSC 2026 is the most structurally important diplomatic development of the past year.
Macron· February 14, 2026 · Munich Security Conference
"Europe must stop being naive. The strategic autonomy I have spoken of for nine years is not a slogan — it is now a survival requirement. We are not waiting for permission."
Analyst note
"We are not waiting for permission" — the furthest Macron has publicly gone. The 2017 Sorbonne vision and the 2026 Munich statement are the same argument — but the second is backed by three years of military commitment.
⟳
The temporal balcony
Step back from the current moment. See the arc. Hold the vision. Find where to work today.
Visualizing victory — Ukraine · Russia · 2026
This is what we are working toward. Keep it in view.
The arc of the conflict
Where things have moved
Putin: "The matter is coming to an end" — May 9, 2026. Most explicit exit-ramp signal yet.
Zelensky: Private territorial flexibility emerging — gap between stated and actual position narrowing.
Trump: 53 "24 hour" promises unfulfilled. Credibility tied to getting a deal.
Macron + Merz: "Independence from the USA" — the most structurally important convergence of 2025-26.
Russia: Victory Day parade visibly scaled back. First domestic signal of resource pressure.
The forward vision
What resolution looks like
Ceasefire and separation of forces along current line of contact
European Peace and Security Presence — not NATO, but armed and with a mandate
Agreed ambiguity on territory — Korean model, not formal recognition
Reconstruction framework — EU integration, frozen assets, phased sanctions relief
The key latent ZOPA: A European force satisfies all five leaders' core needs simultaneously
Open questions
Where to focus today
What does Putin's inner circle actually think about the war's costs? Watch for signals.
3 shifts flagged this week in statements tracker
Can Zelensky sell agreed ambiguity to the Ukrainian military? This is the internal table question.
What is China's actual position after the Beijing summit?
Is the Eastern flank (Poland, Baltics) being brought inside the settlement framework or left outside it?
Balcony question for today
Are there glimmers of movement — however small — that most observers have not noticed?
The Peace Room · Ukraine–Russia Conflict Prototype · Victory Speech Machine · May 2026
